coredata.dc:title: Green synthesized carbon dots embedded in silica molecularly imprinted polymers, characterization and application as a rapid and selective fluorimetric sensor for determination of thiabendazole in juices
coredata.dc:creator: Kazemifard, Nafiseh
Ensafi, Ali A.
Rezaei, Behzad
coredata.dc:description: An eco-friendly method was used to synthesize carbon dots (CDs) from Rosemary leaves, as a carbon source. The as-synthesized CDs was applied as a fluorophore in an optical sensor after modification with molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) for determination of thiabendazole (TBZ). For this purpose, a silica shell using tetraethoxysilane (TEOS), as a Si source, was stabilized on the surface of CDs via reverse microemulsion technique. Following, MIPs were synthesized in the presence of TBZ as a template, using 3-aminopropyl triethoxysilane and TEOS as a functional monomer and a crosslinker, respectively. After optimization of the experimental parameters, a linear dynamic range of 0.03–1.73 μg/mL TBZ with a detection limit as 8 ng/mL were obtained for the suggested method. Finally, the proposed sensor was successfully applied for the determination of TBZ in apple, orange, and tomato juices. This sensor is a simple, rapid, selective, and non-expensive method for TBZ measurement.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Rosemary leaves
Green synthesis carbon dots
Characterization
Thiabendazole
Fluorimetric sensor
Silica molecularly imprinted polymer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CDs@SiO2@MIPs and CDs@SiO2@NIPs nanocomposites
Characterization of the CDs@SiO2@NIPs and CDs@SiO2@MIPs nanocomposites
TBZ determination mechanism via CDs@SiO2@MIPs nanocomposite

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A) Absorption spectrum; B) Fluorescence spectrum; C) FT-IR spectrum; and D) Raman spectrum of the synthesized CDs.
A) XRD pattern; B) EDX analysis; C) TEM image; D) DLS diagram; E) Zeta potential curve; and F) NMR spectrum of the synthesized CDs.
A) SEM image of CDs@SiO2@MIPs nanocomposite; B) SEM image of CDs@SiO2@NIPs nanocomposites; and C) FT-IR of the synthesized nanocomposites.

A) Effect of pHs on the response of CDs@SiO2@MIPs nanocomposite (1.5 mg/mL) to 0.5 μg/mL TBZ solutions at room temperature; B) Effect of temperature on the fluorescence intensity of CDs@SiO2@MIPs nanocomposite (1.5 mg/mL) in the presence of 0.5 μg/mL TBZ solutions (F) and in the absence of TBZ (F0) at pH 7.0; C) Effect of time on the response of CDs@SiO2@MIPs nanocomposite (1.5 mg/mL) to 0.5 μg/mL TBZ solutions at pH 7.0 and room temperature; D)The responses of CDs@SiO2@MIPs nanocomposite and CDs@SiO2@NIPs nanocomposite to 0.4 μg/mL TBZ in the presence of different kinds of pesticides at 50.0 μg/mL; and E) The effect of some common natural ingredients (in the juices, at 50.0 μg/mL) on the response of CDs@SiO2@MIPs nanocomposite and CDs@SiO2@NIPs in the presence of 0.4 μg/mL TBZ.
Comparison of detection limit, linear range and RSD of the suggested method with other reported in the field of TBZ measurement.

row.entry: C18 silica gel as support to retain and preconcentration – fluorescence spectroscopy

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The results of the accuracy and recovery test for real sample analysis.

row.entry: TBZ found (μg/mL)* by the suggested method

head.ce:title: Green synthesized carbon dots embedded in silica molecularly imprinted polymers, characterization and application as a rapid and selective fluorimetric sensor for determination of thiabendazole in juices

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Isfahan University of Technology, Isfahan 84156-83111, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Isfahan University of Technology, Isfahan 84156-83111, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An eco-friendly method was used to synthesize carbon dots (CDs) from Rosemary leaves, as a carbon source. The as-synthesized CDs was applied as a fluorophore in an optical sensor after modification with molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) for determination of thiabendazole (TBZ). For this purpose, a silica shell using tetraethoxysilane (TEOS), as a Si source, was stabilized on the surface of CDs via reverse microemulsion technique. Following, MIPs were synthesized in the presence of TBZ as a template, using 3-aminopropyl triethoxysilane and TEOS as a functional monomer and a crosslinker, respectively. After optimization of the experimental parameters, a linear dynamic range of 0.03–1.73 μg/mL TBZ with a detection limit as 8 ng/mL were obtained for the suggested method. Finally, the proposed sensor was successfully applied for the determination of TBZ in apple, orange, and tomato juices. This sensor is a simple, rapid, selective, and non-expensive method for TBZ measurement.

ce:section.ce:para: Thiabendazole (TBZ) is a benzimidazole fungicide, commonly used as a pre-harvest to control diseases or during storage and transport of different crops (García-Fernández, Díaz-Álvarez, & Martín-Esteban, 2017). TBZ remains stable even in different food preparation processes including pasteurization, cooking, and brewing (Tsialla, Ucles-Moreno, Petrou, Fernandez-Alba, & Kakabakos, 2015). TBZ adsorbs very strongly in soil surface. The half-life of TBZ in soil has been reported as 933 days. On the other hand, TBZ can be decomposed in water under sunlight, with a half-life of about 29 hours. TBZ is stable in frozen crops for 12–28 months and in animal tissue for 2 months or more. The maximum residue levels for TBZ has been set by a competent authority in the range of 0.05–15 mg/kg, depending upon the category of agricultural compounds (García-Fernández et al., 2017). Therefore, fast, sensitive and selective determination of the low concentration of TBZ is necessary.

As yet, several methods have been presented for the determination of TBZ including chemiluminescence method (Sghar, Aqoob, Unawar, & Abi, 2016)), fluorimetry (Huang et al., 2012; Li et al., 2015; Murillo Pulgarín, García Bermejo, & Rodríguez, 2018), capillary electrophoresis (Oliveira, Loureiro, de Jesus, & de Jesus, 2017), solid phase extraction-high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Lin et al., 2013), and HPLC with fluorescence detection after micellar extraction (Halko, Padron Sanz, Sosa Ferrera, & Santana Rodríguez, 2004). In several reports, molecularly imprinting polymers (MIPs) for improvement in the selectivity of TBZ determination were used, including MIPs-Raman spectroscopy (Alsammarraie et al., 2018), MIPs as selective stationary phase in HPLC (Turiel, Tadeo, Cormack, & Martin-Esteban, 2005), MIPs-capillary electrochromatography (Cacho, Schweitz, Turiel, & Pérez-Conde, 2008), and MIPs-solid phase extraction-HPLC (García-Fernández et al., 2017).

MIPs have cavities those are created during the synthesis of the polymer. These cavities are perfectly matched to the template (target analyte) in shape, size, functional groups and orientation of the cavities (Chen, Wang, Lu, Wu, & Li, 2016). MIPs in various fields have received considerable attention because of their advantages such as ease of production, cheapness, and high stability in different experimental conditions such as temperature, pH, and toxic environments (Ensafi, Nasr-Esfahani, & Rezaei, 2018). Because of the limitations of the traditional imprinting techniques, in which bulk polymer is produced, the surface imprinting technique is taken into consideration as a capable alternative. In surface imprinting technique, recognition cavities are located at the surface or at very near the surface of a support. For example, in case of optical sensors, support can be CdTe QDs (Ensafi, Kazemifard, & Rezaei, 2017a, 2017b), FeSe QDs (Li et al., 2018), carbon dots (CDs) (Ensafi et al., 2018), and graphene QDs (Mehrzad-Samarin, Faridbod, Dezfuli, & Ganjali, 2017). Surface imprinting polymers have more surface area, greater binding capacity, faster mass transfer, and better cavities availability. In addition, the template can remove easier with higher efficiency from the polymer (Díaz-Díaz et al., 2012).

Carbon dots (CDs) are a novel kind of carbon nanomaterials with broad absorption spectrum, multiple exciton generations, high photostability, high fluorescent intensity, and high solubility in water and some organic solvents. Also, the emission spectrum of CDs often changes under different excitation wavelengths or type of their surface functional groups (Jahanbakhshi & Habibi, 2016). CDs are less poisonous than semiconductor quantum dots that prepared with heavy metals such as Cd. CDs are more suitable for bioimaging and biomolecule/drug delivery. In recent years, green methods for synthesis of CDs attracted much attention. Green synthesis using nature carbon sources are non-expensive, easy and most importantly eco-friendly (Ensafi, Hghighat Sefat, Kazemifard, Rezaei, & Moradi, 2017).

Rosemary (Rosmarinus officinalis L.) is a subgroup of the mint family. This evergreen aromatic shrub grows in many parts of the world. Rosemary has different chemical compounds, such as caffeic acid, rosmarinic acid, ursolic acid, carnosic acid, carnosol etc. (Ngo, Williams, & Head, 2011). Therefore, it can be used as a green carbon source for the synthesis of CDs. There are various parameters in selecting a carbon source for the synthesis of CDs. One of these parameters is that the carbon source should be rich in hydrocarbons, the second is that it should be available and finally, it has not been used to synthesize CDs in the past because of enhancement of innovation of the work. Rosemary has different hydrocarbons, it is abundantly found, and it was collected from the IUT campus. Rosemary has not previously been used to synthesize CDs. In conclusion, Rosemary was selected as the carbon source in this.

In our laboratory, CDs from rosemary are synthesized and characterized by several methods (Rezaei, Irannejad, Ensafi, & Kazemifard, 2019). In this paper, additional characterization techniques such as Raman and NMR spectroscopy were added. In addition, in this work, the CDs was used as a fluorophore in an optical sensor, after its modification with molecularly imprinted polymers, for the determination of TBZ. According to our knowledge, this work is the first report in the field of determination TBZ by means of a fluorosensor using MIPs.

The nanoparticles were characterized using several spectroscopic techniques including Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectroscopy, transmission electron microscope (TEM), nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy, Raman spectroscopy, energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy, Zeta potential and dynamic light scattering (DLS).

In the present work, the prepared CDs has been used in the fabrication of a new optical sensor that was a silica-based MIPs for the determination of TBZ. To manufacture of the sensor, in the first step, the surface of the CDs was modified with silica shell via reverse microemulsion procedure to get CDs@SiO2 nanocomposite. In the next step, the imprinting surface was applied to the preparation of MIPs on the surface of CDs@SiO2 nanocomposite, to produce CDs@SiO2@MIPs. Combination advantages of MIPs and CDs made a TBZ optical nanosensor with low detection limit and high selectivity.

Rosemary leaves were collected from Isfahan University of Technology campus. Cyclohexane, Triton X-100, NH4OH (25–28%), 3-aminopropyl triethoxysilane (APTES), tetraethoxysilane (TEOS), acetonitrile, acetone, and ethyl alcohol were purchased from Aldrich (London, UK). For the preparation of universal buffer solution, a mixed acidic solution including boric acid (0.04 mol/L), acetic acid (0.04 mol/L) and phosphoric acid (0.04 mol/L) was prepared. Then, suitable amounts of 0.2 mol/L NaOH solution was added drop by drop to the above solution to achieve the desired pH that controlled by pH-meter with a glass electrode.

Jasco FP–750 spectrofluorometer (Tokyo, Japan) and WPA light wave S2000 UV/Vis spectrophotometer were used for the luminescence and absorption spectra recording, respectively. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were carried out (at 4000–400 cm−1, KBr powder‐pressed pellets) on a Jasco 680-plus spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). An XRD pattern of the CDs was recorded using X-Ray diffractometer (D8-Advance Bruker using Cu-Kα radiation, Germany). Transmission electron micrographs (TEM) were carried out on a transmission electron microscope (Philips CM30 300 kV, Netherlands). The CDs were purified using a 10 kDa MW cut-off Amicon Ultra-4 centrifugal filter unit (Millipore, Madrid, Spain). Raman spectrum was recorded on a Raman Microscope (Taksan P50C0R20, IRAN). An energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy (Seron Technology AIS2300 C) was applied for the EDX analysis of the CDs. Zeta potential and DLS were obtained via dynamic light scattering instrument, Malvern ZEN3600 A (UK).

In this work, the CDs were synthesized via a green carbon source (Rosemary leaves). Usually, CDs can be synthesized via different methods such as laser ablation, hydrothermal, microwave, and ultrasonic method (Ma, Ming, Huang, Liu, & Kang, 2012). Among the synthesis methods, hydrothermal/solvothermal treatment has more benefits such as stability, single-step synthesize, rapid, effective, surface states tunable, low cost, and environmentally friendly. Also, in hydrothermal/solvothermal treatment the nanostructure size of the prepared CDs is controllable.

Here, green synthesis was used to prepare the CDs. According to the rules of green chemistry, the use or production of harmful chemicals should be eliminated or minimized. Water and rosemary were all materials, which were used in the hydrothermal synthesis of the CDs. No other chemical agents or hazardous solvents were used.

For the synthesis of the CDs, the collected Rosemary leaves were washed with water to remove any dust. Following, the leaves were washed with distilled water and finally dried at room temperature. The dried leaves were powdered, and 1.0 g of the resulting powder was added to 100 mL water. The obtained mixture was transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave, sealed and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. In order to separate any non-reactive materials from the CDs solution, the product was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. The CDs solution was filtered througha centrifugal filter with 10 kDa MW cut-off and transferred to a dark glass and kept at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CDs@SiO2@MIPs and CDs@SiO2@NIPs nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs@SiO2@MIPs nanocomposite was synthesized via reverse micro-emulsion procedure based on previous reported (Yang & Gao, 2005). Briefly, 5.4 mL of Triton X-100 and 22.5 mL cyclohexane were mixed using magnetic stirring. Then, 1.2 mL CDs solution, 0.15 mL of TEOS and 0.3 mL of NH4OH (25–28% v/v in water) were added into the above mixture. The resulting product was magnetically stirred for 2 h. After adding 0.06 mL of APTES and 15 mg of TBZ, the reaction container was sealed and stirred for 12 h. After this time, the micro-emulsion was broken by using 60 mL of acetone. Finally, the CDs@SiO2@MIPs nanocomposite was separated by centrifugation (10,000 rpm for 10 min). The result solid phase was washed several times with a mixture of acetonitrile/EtOH (2/8 v/v) as far as no TBZ could be detected by the UV–Vis spectrophotometer (in the wavelength range of 200–320 nm). The non-imprinted polymers (NIPs) on the CDs that have been called as CDs@SiO2@NIPs nanocomposite were synthesized according to the same procedure, while no TBZ was added through the polymerization.

To measure TBZ, the CDs@SiO2@MIPs nanocomposite (1.5 mg/mL) was dispersed in the universal buffer (pH 7.0). A notable aspect is that the CDs@SiO2@MIPs nanocomposite was dispersed in the buffer solution by ultrasonic. Dispersing was checked by eye, the results showed that 7 min was enough time for completely dispersof the CDs. Then, 2 mL of the solution was transferred to a fluorescence cell at room temperature. After 1 min, the fluorescence spectra before and after TBZ addition were taken from 320 to 500 nm (λex = 300 nm). The fluorescence intensity at the maximum wavelength, λmax = 360 nm, in the absence of TBZ and presence of TBZ in different concentrations referred to F0 and F, respectively. The result subtracting F and F0, which depends on the concentration of TBZ, was followed as an analytical signal of the proposed method.

Apple, orange and tomato samples were purchased from the local supermarket (Isfahan, Iran). The samples were washed and chopped. Then, to separate suspended particles and large pieces, a 10-mL portion of each of the samples was transferred into a centrifugal tube. The upper phase was used without extra pre-treatment. Each juice sample was diluted (10-fold) with the universal buffer solution (pH 7.0). Then, TBZ was measured according to the suggested procedure. Because the concentration of TBZ in all of the real samples was less than the detection limit of the method, spiked samples and recovery test were used to evaluate the applicability of the method in real samples.
Various techniques were applied to characterize the synthesized CDs, including spectrophotometry, XRD, FT-IR, EDX, TEM, NMR, Raman, DLS, and zeta potential.

Fig. 1 A shows the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the synthesized CDs, there is one sharp peak of π-π* transition at 288 nm, which is usually related to the conjugated CC bond (Mehta, Jha, Basu, Singhal, & Kailasa, 2015). Fig. 1B demonstrates the fluorescence spectrum (upon λex = 380 nm) of the CDs, more details about the origin of fluorescence will bring in the TBZ detection mechanism (Section 3.3).

Fig. 1C shows the FT-IR spectrum of the CDs. Both bands of CO and CC stretching vibrations are placed around 1600 cm−1. The band at 1065 cm−1 can be assigned to CO bending vibration. The absorption band placed at ~3300 cm−1 is related to OH and NH stretching mode. The band at 2930 cm−1 is oriented to the C H stretching vibrations. The peaks placed at 1590 and 1400 cm−1 could correspond to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibration of COO−, respectively. These results indicated that the hydroxyl, carboxylic and amine groups were present at the surface of the synthesized CDs (Yu et al., 2015).

Fig. 1D shows the Raman spectrum of the synthesized CDs. As can be seen in this figure, D band that related to vibration sp3 hybridized was observed at around 1300 cm−1. Another broadband at around 1600 cm−1 is G band that related to vibration of sp2 hybridized. The Raman intensity ratio of the D and G band (ID/IG) can be used to determine the degree of disorder in the sample (Dychalska et al., 2015). In this work ID/IG was about 1.2 that shows the amorphous structure of the CDs.

EDX spectroscopy was applied for the elemental analysis of the synthesized CDs. The result is shown in Fig. 2 A and the inset Table shows the mass parentage that indicates 81.73% of the CDs formed from C (65.57%) and O (16.16%).

XRD pattern of the CDs (Fig. 2B) shows two broad peaks at 2θ = 22° (0 0 2), and 45° (100). This pattern showed a graphene-like aromatic structure and disordered carbon atoms of the CDs (Liu et al., 2017). According to this pattern, there are both (0 0 2) interlayer distance and (1 0 0) in-plane lattice distance in the CDs. Interlayer distance is powerfully influenced by the existence of the attached hydroxyl, epoxy/ether, carbonyl, and carboxylic acid groups. These functional groups can enhance interlayer distance (Li et al., 2013).

Fig. 2C shows the TEM image of the synthesized CDs indicates the diameter of CDs around 5 nm. According to the obtained results from DLS in Fig. 2D, the average size of the synthesized CDs was about 5 nm.

According to the result (Fig. 2E), the zeta potential of the CDs in the aqueous medium was −14 mV. This negative zeta potential can be created by the presence of hydroxyl, carbonyl and carboxyl groups on the surface of the CDs.

The result of the 1H nuclear magnetic resonance (1H NMR) spectroscopy is observable in Fig. 2F. This spectrum shows four kinds of proton peaks around the four regions: 1–3 ppm (sp3 CH protons), 3–6 ppm (the protons of hydroxyl, ether and carbonyl groups), 6–8 ppm (sp2 aromatic protons) and 8–10 ppm (aldehydic protons) (De & Karak, 2013).

Quantum yield of the CDs (Q) was calculated using the following equation:
Q =
Q
′
×
A
′
A
×
I
I
′
×
n
2
n
′ 2
where Q′ is the quantum yield of the reference (0.89 for Rhodamine B in ethanol). I and I′ are fluorescence peaks area of the CDs and the reference under 335 and 500 nm as excitation wavelength, respectively. A and A′ are absorbance of the CDs and the reference at their excitation wavelengths. Finally, n and n′ are refractive indexes of the aqueous CDs solution (=1.33) and ethanolic solution of the reference (=1.36) (Ensafi, Zakery, & Rezaei, 2019). The Q for the CDs was calculated as 0.28 that is better and at least comparable to previous reports for CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the CDs@SiO2@NIPs and CDs@SiO2@MIPs nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3 A and 3B show the scanning electron micrographs (SEM) of CDs@SiO2@MIPs and CDs@SiO2@NIPs nanocomposites, respectively. The morphology and diameter of the CDs@SiO2@NIPs and CDs@SiO2@MIPs nanocomposites are similar, both nanocomposites are sphere-shaped, and their diameters are about 70 nm.

FT-IR spectra of CDs@SiO2@NIPs and CDs@SiO2@MIPs nanocomposites are presented in Fig. 3C. As clearly shown in these spectra there are similar characteristic peaks of CDs@SiO2@NIPs and CDs@SiO2@MIPs nanocomposites. The peaks around 1050 and 790 cm−1 are associated with the asymmetric vibrations of siloxane groups and bending vibration of the Si-O groups, respectively. The bands around 3271 and 3420 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations CH and NH, respectively. These observations prove that the polymerization was done successfully and the presence of TBZ during the polymerization does not disrupt the polymer structure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: TBZ determination mechanism via CDs@SiO2@MIPs nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs are known as organic fluorophores. However, the cause of fluorescence in CDs is not exactly clear, the emission can be attributed to two sources, one of them is related to an intrinsic band gap subsequent from core sp2 conjugation (sp2 clusters) (Yu, Wen, Toh, & Tang, 2012). The fluorescence of this origin is controlled by the π states of the sp2 sites. It is necessary to mention the electronic levels of π and π* from the sp2 clusters depends on the band gap of the σ and σ* states. Radiative recombination of electron-hole pairs in such sp2 clusters is the cause of fluorescence and another source of fluorescence in CDs is related to the surface state. The surface state can be excited in two ways, either directly or indirectly. The surface state indirectly way can be an energy acceptor from an intrinsic band.

According to the previous studies, the surface state plays a more important role in the fluorescence behavior of CDs rather than the intrinsic core state. The presence of some traps on the surface of CDs, defect states and performing non-radiative relaxation processes can reduce fluorescence intensity due to dropping the excited electrons to the ground state without creating a photon. So, for fluorescence enhancement, some process is performed such as passivation, modification of the surface of the CDs and reducing of some groups such as carbonyl, epoxy, and alkylamines connected to the CDs, that can induce the non-radiative recombination (Zhu, Song, Zhao, Shao, Zhang, & Yang, 2015).

According to the result of this work (Fig. 1S-A), TBZ can enhance the fluorescence intensity of the CDs@SiO2@MIPs. The alkylamine group of APTES that is the functional monomer in the MIPs preparation proses can induce the non-radiative of the excited electrons. So, it is realistic that the passivation of these groups by connecting to TBZ can enhance the fluorescence of the CDs. As can be seen in Fig. 1S-A the fluorescence of the nanocomposite increased with increasing of TBZ concentration.

Also, the influence of the concentration of TBZ on the fluorescence intensity of the non-imprinted polymers, CDs@SiO2@NIPs nanocomposite as a blank nanocomposite, was also investigated and the result is shown in Fig. 1S-B.

For a better understanding of the effect of TBZ on the fluorescence intensity of CDs@SiO2@MIPs nanocomposite, a curve of F-F0 vs. concentration of TBZ (μg/mL) was plotted (Fig. 1S-C). Also, Fig. 1S-D is related to CDs@SiO2@NIPs nanocomposite and shows the curve of F-F0 vs. TBZ concentration. The results confirm TBZ has no significant effect on the fluorescence intensity of the CDs@SiO2@NIPs nanocomposite. This results in emphasize the role of recognizing cavities on silica-based MIPs in the detection of TBZ.

The influence of solution pH on the response of the optical sensor to TBZ was investigated. For the pH optimization, responses of 1.5 mg/mL of CDs@SiO2@MIPs nanocomposite to 0.5 μg/mL TBZ at different pHs (3–10) and at room temperature were recorded. The results (Fig. 4 A) confirm that CDs@SiO2@MIPs nanocomposite has the best response to TBZ at pH 7.0. The response of the CDs@SiO2@MIPs nanocomposite was weakened at lower pH because the hydrogen bonding between TBZ and functional monomer decreased. This behavior is easily justified using the chemical structure of TBZ. TBZ has three acidic protons. The pKa1, pKa2, and pKa3 values are –0.5, 4.8 and 11.3, respectively. So, TBZ exists as a cation protonated at pHs < 5, is a neutral molecule at 5 < pHs < 11, and finally it is an anion at pHs > 11 (Huang et al., 2012). At pH values greater than 7, the silica shell is ionized, and the surface defects were created. Therefore, the fluorescence intensity of CDs@SiO2@MIPs also reduced by increasing the solution pH from 7 to greater values (Ensafi et al., 2017a, 2017b).

The influence of temperature on the performance of the optical sensor was studied. To achieve this purpose, 1.5 mg/mL of CDs@SiO2@MIPs nanocomposite in the universal buffer solution (pH 7.0) was dispersed without TBZ addition. Then, the fluorescence intensity of this mixture was recorded at different temperatures (25–50 °C). In a parallel experiment, the same procedure was performed in the presence of 0.5 μg/mL TBZ. According to the results that are brought in Fig. 4B, F0 clearly declines upon increasing temperature; it is due to thermally activated non-radiative trapping such as surface/defect/ionization and impurity trapping (Yu et al., 2012). An interesting point in the temperature investigation is that the temperature did not significantly affect the F. Probably these observations are justifiable by the passivation of the CDs by TBZ. Therefore, room temperature, which is easily accessible selected as an optimum temperature, was used to carry out the next experiments.

Optimization of the response time, as an effective parameter on the determination of TBZ, which is duration time between TBZ addition and the fluorescence measurement, is very important. The response of the CDs@SiO2@MIPs nanocomposite (1.5 mg/mL) to TBZ (0.5 μg/mL) at pH 7.0 and at 25 °C in different times was recorded. As the results clearly showed (Fig. 4C), the practical response time of the CDs@SiO2@MIPs was 1 min. Time has influence on TBZ detection and the recognition of the cavities interactions. Longer time causes increasing the interaction time between TBZ and the MIPs cavities, therefore the fluorescence enhancement was increased (Ensafi et al., 2017a, 2017b). In this work, this interaction time reached balance quickly in 1 min. The sensor response time was short, and this short response time was one of the advantages of this approach.

Under the optimum experimental conditions, the results present a linear dynamic range from 0.03 to 1.73 μg/mL TBZ with a detection limit of 8.0 ng/mL (3Sb/m where “Sb” is the standard deviation of the blank at 10 repeated measurements, and “m” is the slope of the analytical curve).

To evaluate the repeatability of the sensor, the response of the CDs@SiO2@MIPs nanocomposite in two different concentrations of TBZ was recorded three times. The relative standard deviation (RSD%) for 0.3 and 1.1 μg/mL TBZ were obtained as 4.6% and 2.9%, respectively. The reproducibility of the suggested method for the determination of 0.5 μg/mL TBZ, using CDs@SiO2@MIPs nanocomposite on 3 consecutive days was evaluated too. The RSD% was calculated as 6.1%. This result confirmed the satisfactory reproducibility of the CDs@SiO2@MIPs nanocomposite in this period.

The analytical figures of merit for the different TBZ determination are given in Table 1 . As outlined in this Table, the suggested method has better or at least comparable with several reported methods for TBZ based on the linear range, detection limit, and repeatability. Also, other referred methods often time-consuming, costly, and need pre-concentration steps, while the present method does not need these steps.

The selectivity of the CDs@SiO2@MIPs nanocomposite was studied at the optimal experimental conditions by comparing the obtained signal of the CDs@SiO2@MIPs nanocomposite to TBZ (0.4 μg/mL) and other kinds of pesticides, such as difenoconazole, phosalone, diniconazole, oxadiazon, and fenitrothion (all at 50.0 μg/mL) in the presence of TBZ (0.4 μg/mL). The obtained results are shown in Fig. 4D. Also, this figure shows another category of the results related to the selectivity test of the CDs@SiO2@NIPs nanocomposite in the same experimental and concentration conditions. In addition to investigate the effect of different kinds of pesticides on the method selectivity, the influence of some common natural ingredients (in the juices) such as thiamine hydrochloride (vitamin B1), riboflavin (vitamin B2), ascorbic acid (vitamin C), chlorogenic acid, caffeic acid, and some minerals species such as K+, Na+, Mg2+, and Ca2+ (all at 50.0 μg/mL) in the presence of TBZ (0.4 μg/mL) were also examined (Fig. 4E). The results clearly show that the selectivity of the CDs@SiO2@MIPs nanocomposite for TBZ detection is superior better than the CDs@SiO2@NIPs nanocomposite. The reason for this worthy selectivity is the presence of cavities that correctly fitted with TBZ in shape, size and functional groups (Ensafi et al., 2017a, 2017b). On the other hand, non-selectively of the TBZ bound to CDs@SiO2@NIPs nanocomposite is the result of the poor selectivity.
An analytical method is acceptable when it has applicability in the analysis of real samples. The ability of the CDs@SiO2@MIPs nanocomposite to measure TBZ in different juices were investigated.

The linear range of the concentration for TBZ in juices was 0.04–1.7 μg/mL. Because the half-life of TBZ in aquatic media is low (about 29 hours), TBZ had decomposed before getting the lab, this value is even less in the acidic environment such as citrus, which is why the spiked amounts were measured in the juices. The levels selected could cover all linear range of the suggested method, so this method can be used for determination of TBZ in fruit juices in this concentration range. In this work, standard addition method was used to eliminate the matrix effects since the matrix affects all of the solutions equally. This method can be applied in a complex matrix. The results obtained by the suggested method are compared to those obtained by HPLC method, as a standard method. t-student test was used to compare the data obtained via the suggested method and the HPLC results. The results obtained are given in Table 2 . As can be seen in this Table, the proposed method has good accuracy.

In this research, a new approach is demonstrated for TBZ optical sensing. In this method, eco-friendly synthesis CDs was performed using Rosemary as a carbon source. The CDs were applied as a fluorophore probe. One of the biggest weaknesses of the CDs, which limits the application of CDs as a probe in sensors, is their poor selectivity. To overcome this weakness, MIPs with the TBZ recognition cavities were decorated on the surface of the CDs by a simple reverse microemulsion procedure. The suggested method, in addition to being cost-effective and simple, has high sensitivity and selectivity for measuring trace amounts of TBZ.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2019.125812.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
